item.title: Government to enact law on tobacco control
item.description: THE Government will introduce new tobacco control regulations to tackle the country’s high smoking rate and to protect people’s health, according to Health Minister Elias Kapavore.  The new rules are expected to come into effect next month.
item.content:encoded:
THE Government will introduce new tobacco control regulations to tackle the country’s high smoking rate and to protect people’s health, according to Health Minister Elias Kapavore. 

The new rules are expected to come into effect next month.

It will bring the Tobacco Control Act 2016 to full effect.

Kapavore said the measures were intended to reduce tobacco use, which was contributing to about 12 per cent of all deaths (8,500) in PNG each year. 

“Tobacco is killing our people and placing an unacceptable burden on families across the country,” Kapavore said.

“These regulations are about saving lives and protecting the future of PNG.”

More than 40 per cent of adults and nearly one in four young people smoke.

The habit leads to cancer, heart and lung diseases. 

Kapavore estimated the economic cost at more than K787 million annually due to lost productivity and healthcare spending. 

“This is not just a health issue, it is an economic issue and a development issue that is holding our people and our country back,” Kapavore added.

“Too many families are losing loved ones, and too many communities are losing people who should be contributing to our nation’s future”. The regulations were developed with health and legal experts to introduce practical, evidence-based measures.

The Government will require larger graphic health warnings on all tobacco packaging.

Authorities will also enforce stronger rules for smoke-free areas to protect non-smokers.

Furthermore, the regulations will place limits on flavoured tobacco products that make smoking more appealing to young people. 

“These are measures that have worked around the world and will save lives,” Kapavore said.

“They will help reduce smoking, protect our children, and improve the health of our people”. 

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Appointment done through screening, says Duma
item.description: STATE Enterprises Minister William Duma says all chairmen, directors and chief executive officers of State-owned Enterprises (SOE) are appointed through a thorough screening process, and not only by the prime minister or ministers. “We will continue to exercise the policy of non-political interference of our SOEs,” he said.
item.content:encoded:
STATE Enterprises Minister William Duma says all chairmen, directors and chief executive officers of State-owned Enterprises (SOE) are appointed through a thorough screening process, and not only by the prime minister or ministers.

“We will continue to exercise the policy of non-political interference of our SOEs,” he said.

“This Government has done very well by staying away from interfering into all operations of SOEs.

“Even though most of the SOEs are struggling, you can only attribute it to either incompetence, legacy issues a combination of both.”

He was speaking in Port Moresby yesterday during the presentation of K3 million dividend by Datec PNG to Telikom.

He said that as a result of the SOE reforms, “we have made it a condition that all the chairmen and directors of all our SOEs and CEOs are recruited and appointed after they go through a thorough and independent screening process”.

“We have changed the way appointments have been made in the past,” he said.

“We have engaged independent recruitment consultants based overseas. So anyone who wants to apply to be considered for appointment as a chairman or a director in one of the SOEs, or managing directors or CEO of all our SOEs, has to apply to the recruitment consultant based overseas by email.”

Cabinet only comes in after the consultant has submitted a short-list.

“I do not make appointments single-handedly as a minister, not even the prime minister,” he said.

“It is a collective decision by Cabinet based on a thorough, transparent and fair process.”

He said that the Government was the largest customer which had not paid its debts to PNG Power and Water PNG, although the two entities continued to operate.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Airport security confiscate gold bars
item.description: TWENTY gold bars of various sizes were detected by the National Airports Corporation (NAC) aviation security (Avsec) screeners at the Jackson international Airport terminal last week and handed over to the Papua New Guinea Customs, an official says. NAC acting managing director Dominic Kaumu said after detection, Avsec liaised with Air Niugini and PNG Customs to identify one of the passengers.
item.content:encoded:
TWENTY gold bars of various sizes were detected by the National Airports Corporation (NAC) aviation security (Avsec) screeners at the Jackson international Airport terminal last week and handed over to the Papua New Guinea Customs, an official says.

NAC acting managing director Dominic Kaumu said after detection, Avsec liaised with Air Niugini and PNG Customs to identify one of the passengers.

“He was then asked to open his suitcase. Two gold bars were confirmed in his luggage.”

He said the passenger was unable to provide valid documentation authorising the export of the gold bars.

Others gold bars were confiscated from two other suitcases belonging to two passengers bound for Hong Kong. “The other two passengers didn’t show up and failed to board the aircraft, therefore, their suitcases which were still locked, were handed over to PNG Customs,” Kaumu said.

“PNG Customs notified Avsec that the passengers didn’t show up to claim the two suitcases.

“Their luggage was opened in the presence of Customs officers. One suitcase contained 12 gold bars and the other had six.”

Kaumu said similar incidents happened last year, including one where gold particles in five plastic wraps were worn around a passenger’s waist.

“This year, it was eight small pieces of smelted gold in February, six plastics wraps of gold coated in clay and gold nuggets found in capsules last month.”

Kaumu praised the Avsec officers and encouraged them to continue to be vigilant and do their work diligently and with integrity.

Kaumu also commended PNG Custom for the successful conviction of a female passenger who was travelling to Hong Kong.

Securities found gold in plastic packs hidden in a custom-made sports bra with pockets.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Maintain good name of lawyers: Sir Gibbs
item.description: CHIEF Justice Sir Gibbs Salika has urged a group of lawyers admitted to the bar yesterday to “maintain the good name you have”. “Being a lawyer is a big thing and you must not mess that up. There were people practising law who have messed themselves and have ended up in prison,” Sir Gibbs said.
item.content:encoded:
CHIEF Justice Sir Gibbs Salika has urged a group of lawyers admitted to the bar yesterday to “maintain the good name you have”.

“Being a lawyer is a big thing and you must not mess that up. There were people practising law who have messed themselves and have ended up in prison,” Sir Gibbs said.

“They have done bad things and messed themselves up. Be someone that the community, society and the people around you will look up to with respect.”

The 94 Legal Training Institute (LTI) graduates appeared before a 12-man bench of judges at the new National and Supreme Court complex in Waigani for the lawyers’ admission.

Among them were the daughters of Judge Panuel Mogish, PNG Law Society president Hubert Namani, Tewai-Siassi MP Dr Kobby Bomareo, and Kainantu MP William Hagahuno.

Sir Gibbs told the 94 lawyers to be proud of their achievement and to build that reputation with the public.

He said: “You must build yourself a reputation for being fearless. There are some lawyers in this jurisdiction who are fearless and fight for their clients.

“There is no harm in you fighting for your clients.  Your parents and guardians must be proud of your achievement as you are not an ordinary person. You are a lawyer.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Families join Chiefs journey
item.description: THE families of two senior PNG Chiefs officials – head coach Willie Peters and general manager (football) Michael Chammas – are in Port Moresby to visit their new homes and schools, underscoring the club’s belief in engaging families as part of its journey towards NRL entry in 2028. Peters’ wife Kera and son Jackson are expected in Port Moresby this morning, while Chammas’ wife and two children ar
item.content:encoded:
THE families of two senior PNG Chiefs officials – head coach Willie Peters and general manager (football) Michael Chammas – are in Port Moresby to visit their new homes and schools, underscoring the club’s belief in engaging families as part of its journey towards NRL entry in 2028.

Peters’ wife Kera and son Jackson are expected in Port Moresby this morning, while Chammas’ wife and two children arrived yesterday, PNG Chiefs CEO Lorna McPherson confirmed.

In a short WhatsApp message on the Peters, Chammas noted: “Family coming tomorrow. Willie not til end of the year.” Peters remains under contract with Hull Kingston Rovers in England’s Super League.

McPherson described the arrival of Peters’ family as “a significant moment for the Chiefs and country”, adding that the Chammas family will live and work in PNG.

“In PNG, family is central to who we are,” she said. “When a leader joins our organisation, their family becomes part of our story, our community, and our purpose.”

She said Kera and Jackson’s visit reflects the Peters family’s commitment to understanding PNG’s people, culture and values. “Their willingness to come, to see, and to experience life here speaks to the depth of this partnership,” McPherson said.

The club is coordinating their arrival to ensure they feel welcomed. “This is (not only) a courtesy visit (but) the beginning of a relationship grounded in trust, belonging and shared ambition,” she added.

On player recruitment, McPherson said: “I cannot comment at this time except to say we are working on ensuring we have an amazing team that reflects PNG and our aspiration in NRL.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Focus on discipline drives NGI coup
item.description: A STRONG focus on discipline and teamwork paid off for the New Guinea Islands (NGI) Academy, as the boys claimed a hard fought 10-0 win over NCD Academy, while the girls went down narrowly 16 14 in a thrilling contest. Team manager Philip Lunganga praised the boys for their outstanding performance, highlighting a big improvement in attitude and execution.
item.content:encoded:
A STRONG focus on discipline and teamwork paid off for the New Guinea Islands (NGI) Academy, as the boys claimed a hard fought 10-0 win over NCD Academy, while the girls went down narrowly 16 14 in a thrilling contest.

Team manager Philip Lunganga praised the boys for their outstanding performance, highlighting a big improvement in attitude and execution.

“The boys were really outstanding this afternoon,” Lunganga told The National. “They improved from the first game to the second, and now in this final game.

“Last week we were supposed to win, but because of our attitude and discipline, we lost. The quality of our game was still good, but we didn’t finish well.”

Coming into the match against NCD, Lunganga said the team had a clear mindset to correct their mistakes; and they delivered.

“This week we came with the mindset to win, and we made it through. Credit to the boys for everything they have done.

“The discipline on the field was much better this time, and that made a big difference,” he said.

The strong performance has also attracted attention from national selectors, with three NGI players already identified for higher honours.

“We have three of our boys who have been scouted for the PNG Junior Kumuls,” Lunganga confirmed. “I can’t give out their names yet, but they have been drafted.”

He also credited the coaching staff for creating a positive and inclusive environment that allows players to grow.

“I’m really pleased with the coaching staff. It’s all about teamwork. Our coach values everyone’s input, not just the staff, but also the players. They share their ideas on how to play the game.”

Despite the girls’ narrow loss, Lunganga said the overall performance from both teams shows the strength and potential of the NGI Academy programme.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Konedobu lads confident despite player exodus
item.description: THE Kone Storms are confident of defending their premiership this season, despite losing a large number of top players to the Digicel ExxonMobil Cup. Senior grade head coach Garry Mana says the club remains strong, thanks to continued support and a growing pool of young talent ready to step up.
item.content:encoded:
THE Kone Storms are confident of defending their premiership this season, despite losing a large number of top players to the Digicel ExxonMobil Cup.

Senior grade head coach Garry Mana says the club remains strong, thanks to continued support and a growing pool of young talent ready to step up.

In acknowledgement, Mana said: “We want to extend our appreciation to (Moresby South MP) Justin Tkatchenko for his continuous support to the club.”

He revealed that the club received K50,000 in funding for the 2026 season, which would play a key role in their Port Moresby Rugby League campaign.

“His support helps us every year to produce top players. Now many of them are playing in the Digicel Cup,” he said.

Kone Storms have become a strong pathway club, with 11 players from last year’s grand final squad and Governor’s Cup team earning selection into Digicel Cup teams this year.

Mana thanked the scouts and coaching staff, including the Port Moresby Vipers, for recognising the talent within the club.

“Big thanks to the Digicel Cup teams and, especially, the Port Moresby Vipers for selecting our boys,” he said.

While losing key players is a challenge, Mana believes it also opens doors for the next generation.

“Yes, it will affect us, but we are preparing as normal. We have many Under-20 boys coming up,” he added.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Ex-Guria guides academy to winning start
item.description: FORMER East New Britain Gurias halfback Longinus Daungia has made a strong start to his new chapter in rugby league, guiding the New Guinea Islands (NGI) Academy to an impressive 10-0 win over Team NCD at the Sir John Guise Stadium on Sunday. Now serving as NGI Academy head trainer, Daungia said the move from player to mentor is both exciting and challenging.
item.content:encoded:
FORMER East New Britain Gurias halfback Longinus Daungia has made a strong start to his new chapter in rugby league, guiding the New Guinea Islands (NGI) Academy to an impressive 10-0 win over Team NCD at the Sir John Guise Stadium on Sunday.

Now serving as NGI Academy head trainer, Daungia said the move from player to mentor is both exciting and challenging.

“It’s quite exciting this year joining the NGI Academy as head trainer. This is my first opportunity in rugby league not as a player, but as a trainer,” he told The National.

After retiring from the ENB Gurias in 2019 due to injury, Daungia has embraced his new role with passion, focusing on developing young talents coming through the ranks. “I am really happy to share my experience and knowledge with these young boys,” he said.

“We have a lot of talented players who just need proper mentoring, grooming and guidance at this level.”

Despite the strong result, Daungia admitted the transition into coaching has not been easy, especially with how much the game has evolved in recent years.

“It’s not a smooth ride. I’ve faced many challenges. The game is growing, and things have changed since I played from 2015 to 2019,” he explained.

“These kids are already advanced in how they think and play, so I have to adjust and keep up. But I am learning from them as well.”

Daungia praised his players for their effort and improvement, especially in their shutout win over hosts NCD.

“Credit to my boys; their performance was very good. Every player improved.

“It’s a positive start for us and I am determined to continue building confident and skilled players for the future of rugby league in the country.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Starlink paves way for connectivity
item.description: THE approval by National Information and Communications Technology Authority for Starlink, operated by SpaceX, offers Papua New Guinea a practical pathway to expand internet access nationwide. Unlike existing providers such as Digicel, Vodafone, and Telikom PNG, it can serve remote and rural areas without relying on towers or fibre networks.
item.content:encoded:
THE approval by National Information and Communications Technology Authority for Starlink, operated by SpaceX, offers Papua New Guinea a practical pathway to expand internet access nationwide.

Unlike existing providers such as Digicel, Vodafone, and Telikom PNG, it can serve remote and rural areas without relying on towers or fibre networks.

In the national interest, the key issue is affordability for ordinary households.

A shared national access model is essential to keep costs low while expanding coverage.

Government should negotiate bulk national pricing to reduce monthly fees and introduce targeted subsidies for low-income families, especially in rural communities.

Each Starlink installation should operate as a community digital hub located at schools, health centres, and ward offices, providing shared Wi-Fi access to nearby households at very low cost.

This spreads expenses across many users, making access affordable at village level.

Further cost reduction can be achieved through tax exemptions on equipment and provincial co-funding arrangements.

In this model, Starlink becomes a shared public digital utility, supporting education, health services, business activity, and disaster response.

It ensures digital inclusion, reduces inequality, and strengthens national development in a practical and affordable way for all Papua New Guineans.

THIS bold, forward-thinking policy decision is exactly what Papua New Guinea has been waiting for.

For far too long, geography has limited opportunity.

Our rural communities, islands, Highlands villages, and remote districts have been cut off from reliable internet because fibre and towers couldn’t reach them.

This isolation has real costs for education, healthcare, small businesses, disaster response, and governance.

National Information and Communications Technology Authority (Nicta)’s approval of operating and spectrum licences for Starlink Internet Services PNG Ltd directly addresses this gap.

Satellite broadband doesn’t require roads or power grids – it can bring high-speed internet to places such as Telefomin, Kiriwina, and Bougainville overnight.

This is universal connectivity in action.

This move aligns with the Government’s digital transformation agenda.

Reliable internet can’t be limited to Port Moresby and Lae.

To build a digital economy, promote financial inclusion, and foster small-to-medium enterprises growth, every district must be online.

Starlink provides the infrastructure to achieve this much faster than traditional rollouts.

What’s more, this is a deliberate policy intervention.

Nicta and the Ministry for Information and Communications Technology understand that connectivity is a cornerstone of development.

Licensing Starlink adds competition, lowers prices, and improves service quality.

Though implementation will be key, today, PNG is already much more connected.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: K1 billion stabilises prices and combats inflation
item.description: THE Government has allocated K1 billion to stabilise energy prices in response to global volatility caused by geopolitical conflicts. Rising energy costs are driving inflation, eroding business confidence, and straining household budgets.
item.content:encoded:
THE Government has allocated K1 billion to stabilise energy prices in response to global volatility caused by geopolitical conflicts.

Rising energy costs are driving inflation, eroding business confidence, and straining household budgets.

Price stabilisation is a key tool in the Government’s fiscal and monetary policies, helping to shield the domestic economy from global price shocks.

By mitigating inflation and protecting purchasing power, this policy fosters economic stability and business confidence, essential for growth.

This approach also addresses trade disruptions, where “dumping” can occur.

When excess capacity results in cheap imports, the Government can raise import taxes, generating revenue to fund public services and reduce business competition.

By stabilising essential goods prices and supporting domestic industries, price stabilisation also protects low-income earners and promotes long-term economic growth. However, successful implementation requires careful planning to ensure it aligns with broader government objectives.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: QR codes set standard
item.description: I WRITE to congratulate the University of Papua New Guinea vice-chancellor for introducing the quick response (QR) barcode on certificates for graduating students this year. The problem of fraudulent certificates has being ongoing for many years and was recently highlighted by the Defence Force recruitment saga, under investigation.
item.content:encoded:
I WRITE to congratulate the University of Papua New Guinea vice-chancellor for introducing the quick response (QR) barcode on certificates for graduating students this year.

The problem of fraudulent certificates has being ongoing for many years and was recently highlighted by the Defence Force recruitment saga, under investigation.

This initiative will help curb fraud as persons will be able to scan the QR barcode and instantly verify the owners photo and necessary information.

This should now be made mandatory for QR barcodes on all certificates issued by primary schools, high schools, universities and government departments.

This would greatly reduce the fraud and restore trust in official documents.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Legal clarity needed on Goroka’s land
item.description: THE Tranche Three Sustainable Highlands Highway Improvement Programme (SHHIP), funded by the Asian Development Bank (ADB), is finally taking shape for Goroka and its people. However, land compensation issues remain unresolved.
item.content:encoded:
THE Tranche Three Sustainable Highlands Highway Improvement Programme (SHHIP), funded by the Asian Development Bank (ADB), is finally taking shape for Goroka and its people.

However, land compensation issues remain unresolved.

The policy framework for land acquisition and resettlement is governed by PNG laws and ADB Safeguards, including the 1996 Land Act, the 2000 Land Disputes Settlement Act, and others.

The land compensation claim for the Goroka corridor remains pending, with no lawful acquisition evidence from the Department of Lands and Physical Planning or the National Housing Corporation

The 1987 declaration of the land as State land violated the Land Act in three critical ways: no landowner consent, failure to follow compulsory acquisition procedures, and lack of documentation.

According to the Land Act, land subject to a customary claim cannot be deemed State land until due procedures are followed, including compensation.

The Goroka land acquisition is legally void, and the Departments of Lands and Physical Planning and Works and Highways must consult with the Hanazuha, Gamazuha, and Asarozuha customary landowners, pay just compensation, and address overlapping claims with neighbouring districts.

The time for action is now – immediate consultation and resolution are needed to move forward with the SHHIP project.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Building support system for victims of violence in Jiwaka
item.description: My whole village turned against me after a single accusation. I ran for my life. I cannot go home. I cannot reclaim my land. If I go back, they will kill me.” In Papua New Guinea’s Highlands, that is not a rare horror.
item.content:encoded:
My whole village turned against me after a single accusation. I ran for my life. I cannot go home. I cannot reclaim my land. If I go back, they will kill me.”

In Papua New Guinea’s Highlands, that is not a rare horror.

It is a lived reality for women caught in the grip of sorcery accusation-related violence, where the attack itself is often only the beginning.

As for survivors of domestic abuse and sexual violence in the area, after the violence comes another fight: finding care quickly, finding it safely, and finding it before fear, stigma and silence close in.

PNG’s latest Demographic and Health Survey (2016-2018) found that 56 per cent of women aged 15 to 49 had experienced physical violence, while 28 per cent  had experienced sexual violence.

Among ever-married women, 63 per cent  had experienced emotional, physical or sexual violence by a spouse.

Even then, many survivors remain silent.

Thirty-nine per cent  of women who had experienced physical or sexual violence had neither sought help nor told anyone.

In the Highlands, the burden is even heavier, with 31.8 per cent  of women aged 15 to 49 reporting sexual violence.

Sorcery accusation-related violence adds another layer of terror.

A 2024 paper by the PNG National Research Institute recorded 1,039 cases affecting 1,554 persons across four provinces between 2016 and 2020.

Jiwaka is considered one of the country’s four hotspots.

Much of this violence never reaches formal reporting systems.

Many of those targeted are women and girls.

In Jiwaka, Médecins Sans Frontières (MSF) works alongside the Provincial Health Authority to expand access to safe, confidential, respectful and free care for survivors of all forms of violence, and to strengthen essential health services across the province.

To strengthen the quality of care and improve community access to essential services, teams assessed health facilities across all three districts to identify gaps in service delivery and priority areas for support.

Based on these findings, MSF delivered several training sessions in nine facilities and trained 164 health workers from September to December last year, aiming to improve clinical capacity and ensure safer, more consistent care.

To increase public awareness and help communities better navigate available services, teams also conducted 245 health talk sessions in Anglip-South Waghi communities, including Minj Health Centre, reaching 1,998 persons while village health volunteers then led nearly 150 community sessions reaching 2,239 persons.

In September last year, the Family Support Centre at Minj began operating with MSF support, providing survivors with a clearer path from first medical care to follow-up appointments.

In cases of rape or severe assault, time matters.

In the first 72 hours, a survivor may need treatment for injuries, emergency contraception, HIV prevention, protection from infection and urgent psychosocial support.

That is why the Family Support Centre matters.

It is meant to make those first hours count and reduce the risk that survivors are lost between services.

“A referral pathway is not a chart on a wall,” said Rachel Wehrung, MSF project coordinator.

“It is a promise that a survivor will not be sent from place to place, forced to repeat her story, relive her trauma, and face the same backlash again.

“If that promise is broken, the pathway becomes another form of harm.”

A referral pathway, in plain terms, is whether help actually leads somewhere.

Whether a woman in crisis meets someone trained, discreet and ready to act, or is left to carry her trauma from office to office until exhaustion, fear or shame takes over.

In Jiwaka, that first link is often not a doctor.

“I became a village health volunteer because I have seen what violence does to our sisters and mothers,” said Yen, a local volunteer.

item.title: Two-award recipient Margaret declares:With God, nothing is impossible
item.description: MARGARET Stanley dedicated the two awards she received at the University of Papua New Guinea School of Business and Public Policy’s graduation on Thursday to her parents for their sacrifice, and to her siblings who expect her to lead by example. “I carry the weight of what my parents went through. So I have come this far because I know that my parents are looking up to me. And my siblings are also
item.content:encoded:
MARGARET Stanley dedicated the two awards she received at the University of Papua New Guinea School of Business and Public Policy’s graduation on Thursday to her parents for their sacrifice, and to her siblings who expect her to lead by example.

“I carry the weight of what my parents went through. So I have come this far because I know that my parents are looking up to me. And my siblings are also looking up to me, plus my family members too. I made it to university, and I need to lead by example.”

Margaret, 23, received the Dean’s Award and the Human Resource Management top-student award. She graduated with a Bachelor of Business Management degree.

She is from Bwagaoia, Alhoga and Gulewa villages of Samari-Murua district in Milne Bay. She is the eldest of three children.

“My dad is a senior pastor at the CRC (Christian Revival Crusade) church, and my mother is one of the staff there, doing church work as well.”

She regards herself fortunate to have such a strong Christian upbringing.

“One thing that I’m always proud of is that God sees me through all of these challenges. It was all because of Him and through the help of the Holy Spirit.”

She appreciates God’s blessings plus her parents’ mentoring duties.

“It was through His help, plus my parents always imparting their wisdom and their knowledge. Although they are limited in that space, I still enjoy their guidance and listen to them.”

Margaret chose the course because she wants to serve the people just like her parents.

“When I was in high school, I chose Social Science as I wanted to study international relations. However, Covid-19 hit and I had to change my course. What course can I take that would align with what I want and would not take me away from my parents?

“I looked at my strength and what I love doing. My parents meet people, get to know people, understand their situations and preach.

“In the work space, that’s human resource management. That’s why I chose human resource management.”

Her biggest challenge at university was peer pressure.

“I thank the Lord and my parents for their corrections and guidance. I would not have set my boundaries and know when to go and when not to go.”

“One thing that I’m always proud of is that God sees me through all of these challenges. It was all because of Him and through the help of the Holy Spirit.”

“My mum gives me advice and wisdom. My dad also gives me advice and corrects me as well, and sets boundaries.”

She is currently attached with a financial institution through its graduate development programme.

Her advice to young people is to remain focused.

“You can have fun but stay focused. Know where your priorities are and always put God first.”

She was “nerve-cited” when her name was called during the graduation.

“I was nervous standing up where everybody’s watching me. I was excited as well because that’s the goal that I’ve been working towards.”

She knew the sacrifices and commitments had paid off when she received the awards.

“In all honesty, and not to sound boastful, it was the years of dedication, sacrifices and also understanding what matters most. Especially in university with so many distractions and peer pressure, you have to understand how to balance that out.

“And I had confidence in myself that I’ll receive the award. I didn’t want to sound too overconfident, but understanding how far I’ve come and what I’ve done and the effort that I put in, I was definitely going to get that award.”

“When I look at it, or when my siblings look at it, they know that Margaret didn’t do it alone. She did it with the help of God.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Rachael dedicates 3 awards to parents and family
item.description: RACHAEL Onopia dedicated the three awards she received yesterday at the University of Papua New Guinea School of Business and Public Policy graduation in Port Moresby to her eldest sister Samantha and late father. “My Dad was a police officer. He died when I was in Grade Nine. That really pushed me to focus on my studies. And my Mum, she’s not working but supports me in whatever way she can.”
item.content:encoded:
RACHAEL Onopia dedicated the three awards she received yesterday at the University of Papua New Guinea School of Business and Public Policy graduation in Port Moresby to her eldest sister Samantha and late father.

“My Dad was a police officer. He died when I was in Grade Nine. That really pushed me to focus on my studies. And my Mum, she’s not working but supports me in whatever way she can.”

Rachael, 22, is from Kasuo village in Chuave, Chimbu. She is the youngest of three sisters.

She graduated with a Bachelor of Accounting degree. She received an award from the Certified Practicing Accountants (CPA), the Vice-Chancellor’s award, and for being the top student in the Accounting division.

She graduated with a Diploma in Accounting in 2023.

She had wanted to study law but during her foundation year found that accounting was more appealing. It also had a little to do with finance.

“Honestly, accounting wasn’t my first choice. It was law. But because of financial constraint, I had to choose a business course.

“And while doing my business management foundation year, accounting was really challenging. It captivated me. So I want something challenging.”

She attributed her success to good time management.

“Key takeaways are consistency and time management. When we are consistent in something, the habit builds up over time. And when we manage our time properly to invest in things that will benefit us, we reap the benefits. And when we put in the hard work, God blesses us.”

Rachael was elated when she found out that she was going to graduate. 

“I mean, it was really exciting when I saw my name on the list.”

She was not expecting any special awards as well.

“But like I said, hard work pays off, plus God’s blessing.”

Her biggest challenge at the university was trying to pass two of the “hardest courses” – advance financial accounting and reporting one and two.

Rachael views education as the key to a better future.

“I’d say I had no one to turn to. With that idea in mind, my education was my only way out. So I invested everything I could in it.”

Rachael describes her eldest sister Samantha as her role model.

“She’s always been very supportive of me. She does not question whatever decision I make. She just supports me. And she goes out of her way to support me financially – and her husband as well.”

I’d say I have no one to turn to. So with that idea in mind, my education is my only way out. So I invested everything I could in it.”

Her strength lies in getting things done under tight deadlines, getting her priorities right, and never giving up.

“Nothing is impossible. You do your best and sometimes the benefits will exceed your expectations. Be disciplined and consistent. They pay off.”

She is now attached with an accounting firm through its graduate development programme.

Eldest sister Samantha, who flew in from West New Britain to attend the graduation ceremony yesterday, is proud of her younger sibling.

“It was hard. Every time when it was time to pay tuition fees, it was some kind of pressure. But God came through for us, every time. Even at the last minute, God is always there providing for our needs. God put people in our lives to see us through. So all thanks to God and my husband’s family. I just wish Dad was here.”

“Firstly, I dedicate my degree to my dad because he always believes in me. When I was still in elementary, he would say: ‘You are going to university. You are going to graduate.’ And here I am.

“Secondly, I want to dedicate all these achievements to my eldest sister and her husband who supported me financially throughout my university life. This is for them and my family who prayed for me constantly.

 “To say that I’m proud is an understatement.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.
